---
title: "AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    code-fold: false
lightbox: true
---

![ALMA image of the protoplanetary disk around AS 209 showing multiple narrow rings and gaps that may indicate ongoing planet formation. Credit: ALMA (ESO/NAOJ/NRAO); A. Sierra (U. Chile).](images/as_209_ficha.png){#fig-as209 fig-align="center" width="650"}

As shown in @fig-as209, the AS 209 disk exhibits a sequence of bright rings and dark gaps in the dust continuum emission.

## Object names

| Catalog | Name |
|---|---|
| Primary name | AS 209 |
| Other designations | — |
| Region | Ophiuchus star-forming region |

## Location

This system is located in the **Ophiuchus molecular cloud**, a star-forming region that hosts many young stellar systems. Specifically, the location of this system is described as:

“AS 209 (M⋆ = 0.9 M⊙, spectral type K5, L⋆ = 1.5 L⊙) is part of the young Ophiuchus star forming region at a distance of 126 pc from the Sun” [@fedele2018, p. 2].

Its distance of **126 pc from Earth** makes it an accessible object for **high-resolution observations using millimeter interferometry**.

## General system properties

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Distance | 126 pc |
| Age | ~0.5–1 Myr |
| Spectral type | K5 |
| Stellar temperature | 4250 K |
| Stellar mass | ~0.9 M☉ |
| Stellar luminosity | ~1.5 L☉ |
| Disk radius | ~200 AU |
| Disk inclination | ~35° |

## Observations

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Instrument | ALMA |
| ALMA band | Band 6 |
| Observing frequency | ~230 GHz |
| Wavelength | 1.3 mm dust continuum |
| Observation date | 22–26 September 2016 |
| Number of antennas | 38–41 |
| Integration time | ~40 min per execution block |
| System temperature | 80–145 K |
| Precipitable water vapour | 2.2–2.3 mm |
| Peak flux | 13 mJy beam⁻¹ |
| RMS noise | 0.1 mJy beam⁻¹ |

## Disk geometry

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Disk inclination | 35.3° |
| Position angle | 86° |
| Synthesized beam | 0.19″ × 0.14″ |

## Disk morphology

ALMA observations reveal a structured disk with multiple rings and gaps in the millimeter dust continuum emission. Two prominent rings are located at approximately **75 AU** and **130 AU**, separated by two narrow gaps at **62 AU** and **103 AU**.

## Morphology measurements

| Structure | Type | Radius (AU) |
|---|---|---|
| G1 | Dark gap | 62 |
| R1 | Bright ring | 75 |
| G2 | Dark gap | 103 |
| R2 | Bright ring | 130 |

## Gap properties

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Gap 1 half width | ~8 AU |
| Gap 2 half width | ~16 AU |

## Disk size

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Outer dust emission | ~170–180 AU |

## Evidence of planet formation

Hydrodynamic simulations of planet–disk interactions indicate that the ringed structure observed in the AS 209 disk can be explained by the presence of a forming giant planet embedded in the disk. The best agreement between simulations and observations is obtained for a **Saturn-mass planet** with a mass of approximately **0.2 Jupiter masses (≈0.67 Saturn masses)** located at a distance of about **95 AU** from the central star.

This planet is capable of producing a deep outer gap in the dust distribution while simultaneously inducing a second inner gap through its gravitational interaction with the surrounding disk material. The structure of the gaps depends strongly on the viscosity of the disk, and the simulations suggest that the disk of AS 209 likely has **very low turbulence**, corresponding to a viscosity parameter **α ≤ 10⁻⁴**.

The radial positions of the two observed gaps (62 AU and 103 AU) are close to the orbital spacing expected for planets in a **2:1 orbital resonance**, which raises the possibility that a second, lower-mass planet could also be present in the inner gap. Simulations show that such a planet would need to have a mass **below ~0.1 Jupiter masses** in order to remain consistent with the observed gap widths.

Radiative transfer modeling also indicates that the presence of a giant planet can create a **pressure maximum in the gas disk**, which traps drifting dust grains and produces the bright rings observed in millimeter continuum emission. Observations of the **C¹⁸O molecular emission** show extended gas emission peaking near **130 AU**, spatially coincident with the outer dust ring. This suggests that the gas surface density follows the large-scale structure of the dust disk and supports the interpretation that the observed rings and gaps are produced by planet–disk interactions.

Taken together, these results indicate that the substructures observed in AS 209 are consistent with the presence of a **forming giant planet in the outer disk**, making this system an important laboratory for studying the early stages of planet formation at large orbital distances.

## Activity

**Coming soon**

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
